summary: The author achieved 9th place using a straightforward 3D segmentation pipeline centered on a customized ConvNeXt-like model. Key insights included scaling ground truth mask radii per particle type to align with the centroid evaluation metric, and modifying the encoder's stem size, kernel dimensions, and block depths to better capture small particles while maintaining inference speed. The pipeline concluded with centroid extraction via cc3d, DBSCAN filtering, and an ensemble of multiple models combined with rotation-based test-time augmentations.
extensive_summary:
## Segmentation & Mask Generation
The solution relies on a straightforward 3D segmentation approach. A critical insight was adjusting the ground truth mask radii for each particle type to align with the competition's centroid evaluation metric, which requires predictions to fall within `r×0.5` of the ground truth. The author applied specific scaling factors per particle class (e.g., `60/2` for apo-ferritin, `150/3` for ribosome), which yielded a public leaderboard score improvement of 0.02–0.04.

## Model Architecture
The core model is a 3D ConvNeXt-like architecture with custom modifications to the encoder and decoder. The encoder adapts the original ConvNeXt for small particle detection by converting all convolutional layers to 3D, reducing the stem size from 4x4 to 2x2, shrinking the conv block kernel from 7x7 to 3x3, and reducing the block depths from `(3, 3, 9, 3)` to `(3, 3, 3, 3)` to improve inference speed without hurting cross-validation performance. The decoder follows a U-Net style flow but replaces `nn.ConvTranspose3d` with `nn.Upsample` (trilinear) to reduce parameters, as the ground truth masks are spherical. Custom `ConvBlock3D` modules use GroupNorm and GELU activations.

## Training & Inference Details
Training utilized a window size of `(32, 256, 256)` with 3D augmentations including `rot90`, `180`, `270`, and `xyz` flips. Inference employed a sliding window of `(32, 320, 320)` with a 6-voxel overlap on the z-axis. Preprocessing was limited to percentile-based normalization (clipping at 1st and 99th percentiles, then min-max scaling). The loss function was binary cross-entropy (BCE).

## Post-Processing & Ensembling
Predicted segmentation masks were processed using `cc3d` to compute particle centroids, followed by DBSCAN filtering to curate the final predictions. The final submission was generated by ensembling as many models as possible, combined with rotation-based test-time augmentations (rot90, 180, 270).
competition_flow: Raw 3D volumetric data is normalized and windowed for training, fed into an ensemble of customized 3D ConvNeXt-like segmentation models, and post-processed via cc3d centroid extraction and DBSCAN filtering to produce final particle coordinates.
data_processing: Percentile-based normalization (clipping at 1st and 99th percentiles, then min-max scaling)
Ground truth mask radii scaled per particle class (e.g., r/2 or r/3)
ensembling: The final submission is an ensemble of multiple trained models, with predictions post-processed via cc3d for centroid computation and DBSCAN filtering, combined with rotation-based TTAs.
insights: Adjusting ground truth mask radii to align with the centroid evaluation metric yields measurable score improvements.
Reducing the encoder stem size and kernel dimensions improves small particle detection without harming cross-validation performance.
Replacing nn.ConvTranspose3d with nn.Upsample effectively reduces model parameters for spherical segmentation tasks.
what_did_not_work: Pretraining with synthetic data provided by the host
Using excessively smaller mask scaling factors (e.g., 0.25 for large particles, 0.4 for small particles)
Using flip TTA instead of rotation TTA

other_solution_refs.description: Reference notebook that served as the starting point for the solution
